title: Waves, Light & Sound
objective: Students will explore waves, light & sound concepts including How We See Objects, Waves & How They Move, Wave Behaviour Vocabulary and more.

steps.content: Let's explore How We See Objects! Have you ever wondered about this?
Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen
Let's practice together! Draw a diagram showing light source → light hits object → reflects into eye
Can you draw a diagram showing how light bounces off a book and enters your eye, which is how you actually see the book?

Great job! Today we learned about How We See Objects. Draw a diagram showing light source → light hits object → reflects into eye Explain that we see objects because reflected light enters our eyes, not because our eyes send out light
Let's discover the amazing world of Waves & How They Move!
Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength, and understand that waves can cause objects to move
Time to try this out: Describe a wave using the terms amplitude (height) and wavelength (distance between peaks)
Can you use a slinky or rope to show how waves work, and explain that bigger waves have more energy and can move things more?

Great job! Today we learned about Waves & How They Move. Describe a wave using the terms amplitude (height) and wavelength (distance between peaks) Demonstrate that larger amplitude means more energy (bigger waves move objects more)
Today we're going to learn about Wave Behaviour Vocabulary. Get ready for an adventure!

Use technical vocabulary for wave behaviour — refraction, absorption, reflection, scattering, amplitude, frequency, wavelength, echo, spectrum, angle of incidence, angle of reflection — and apply these when explaining how light and sound travel and interact with different materials
Let me show you something cool. Use 'refraction' correctly to explain why a straw appears bent in a glass of water
Can you explain why a straw looks bent in a glass of water using a proper science word — or tell you what's actually happening when you hear an echo?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Wave Behaviour Vocabulary: Use 'refraction' correctly to explain why a straw appears bent in a glass of water Distinguish 'reflection' from 'refraction' using the correct definitions
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Patterns & Codes for Information!
Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information, such as codes and signals
Let's practice together! Describe at least two systems that use patterns of light or sound to transfer information (e.g. Morse code, semaphore, drum signals)
Can you come up with different ways to send a secret message using patterns of light or sound, like Morse code or drum beats?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Patterns & Codes for Information: Describe at least two systems that use patterns of light or sound to transfer information (e.g. Morse code, semaphore, drum signals) Design a simple code using light or sound patterns to send a message
